content:
5 min read
Just now

--

As I continue studying Designing Data-Intensive Applications by Martin Kleppmann, I've reached one of the most fascinating topics in distributed systems: Multi-Leader Replication.
At first, replication seems straightforward—you simply copy data from one database to another. However, once applications become global and users are spread across multiple continents, replication becomes much more than copying data. It becomes a problem of latency, availability, fault tolerance, and consistency.
This chapter completely changed how I think about distributed databases.
The Limitation of a Single Leader
Most databases use leader-follower replication.
In this architecture:
One database server is the Leader.
All write requests (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) go to the leader.
Other servers, called Followers, copy data from the leader and mainly serve read requests.
For many applications, this architecture works very well.
But imagine a global application.
Your leader database is located in the United States, while users are located in:
Nigeria
South Africa
Germany
Japan
Brazil
Australia
Every single write must travel to the USA before it is accepted.
Although reads can happen locally from nearby replicas, writes still depend on a single location.
This creates several problems:
Higher write latency
Dependency on one datacenter
Poor user experience during network interruptions
This is exactly the problem Multi-Leader Replication attempts to solve.
What Is Multi-Leader Replication?
Instead of having one leader, multiple datacenters each have their own leader.
For example:
🇺🇸 USA → Leader
🇿🇦 South Africa → Leader
🇩🇪 Germany → Leader
🇯🇵 Japan → Leader
Users write to the nearest leader.
Each leader accepts writes independently.
Later, every leader asynchronously sends its updates to all the other leaders.
From the user's perspective, writes are much faster because they are handled locally.
Advantages of Multi-Leader Replication
1. Lower Write Latency
A user in Nigeria no longer needs to wait for every write to travel across the Atlantic Ocean.
Instead, the request can be processed by the nearest regional leader.
The synchronization with other continents happens in the background.
2. Better Availability
Suppose the USA datacenter suddenly goes offline.
With a single leader, the entire application may temporarily lose its ability to accept writes until another leader is elected.
With Multi-Leader Replication:
Europe keeps working.
Africa keeps working.
Asia keeps working.
Each region continues operating independently.
3. Better Network Fault Tolerance
Internet connections between continents are never perfect.
Temporary network failures happen.
Since every region already has its own leader, users can continue working even while regions are temporarily disconnected.
Once connectivity returns, the leaders synchronize again.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Real-World Use Cases
Multi-Leader Replication is particularly useful for applications that cannot rely on constant connectivity.
Examples include:
Global enterprise applications
Calendar synchronization
Mobile applications
Offline-first software
Collaborative editing platforms like Google Docs
In many ways, every mobile phone can be viewed as its own small "datacenter."
The phone accepts writes locally and synchronizes later whenever an internet connection becomes available.
The Biggest Challenge: Write Conflicts
The biggest downside of Multi-Leader Replication is write conflicts.
Imagine two users editing the same wiki page simultaneously.
User in the USA changes the title:
> A → B
At the exact same time,
User in South Africa changes the title:
> A → C
Both local leaders immediately accept their writes.
Later, replication begins.
Now the system discovers something unexpected.
The same data now has two different values.
Which one should become the final version?
This is called a write conflict.
Why Conflict Resolution Is Difficult
Unlike single-leader replication, there is no universal order of writes.
Every datacenter believes its own write happened first.
The database now has several possible choices.
Last Write Wins (LWW)
The newest write replaces every previous write.
Simple, but it can silently lose data.
Merge Both Versions
Instead of choosing one value, combine both.
For example:
B / C
This preserves information but may not always make sense.
Keep Both Versions
Store every conflicting version.
The application or user later decides which version should become the final result.
Custom Conflict Resolution
Applications can define their own business rules.
For example:
Banking software may reject conflicting transfers.
Shopping carts may merge products.
Document editors may merge text.
Different applications require different strategies.
Replication Topologies
Another interesting concept introduced in this chapter is Replication Topology.
A topology describes how leaders communicate with one another.
Common topologies include:
All-to-All
Every leader communicates directly with every other leader.
Excellent fault tolerance.
More network traffic.
Ring (Circular)
Each leader sends updates to only one neighboring leader.
Simpler.
However, if one node fails, replication may stop until the topology is repaired.
Star
One central leader forwards updates to every other leader.
Easy to manage.
However, the center becomes a single point of failure.
Tree
Updates travel through parent-child relationships.
Useful for large deployments.
More complex to manage.
A Bigger Lesson
One of the biggest lessons I learned from this chapter is that replication is not simply copying data.
Distributed systems must also answer difficult questions such as:
What happens when two users update the same data simultaneously?
What happens if datacenters lose connectivity?
How should conflicting updates be resolved?
How do we preserve the correct order of events?
How do we maintain consistency without sacrificing availability?
These are engineering trade-offs with no perfect answers.
Every architecture chooses a different balance.
Final Thoughts
Before studying distributed systems, I assumed databases mainly stored and retrieved data.
The deeper I go into Designing Data-Intensive Applications, the more I realize databases are sophisticated distributed systems constantly balancing:
Performance
Availability
Consistency
Durability
Fault tolerance
Every chapter reshapes how I think about backend engineering and large-scale system design.
And this is only Chapter 5.
I'm looking forward to learning how leaderless replication, partitioning (sharding), transactions, and distributed consensus solve even more complex challenges in modern distributed systems.
I'm documenting my journey through Designing Data-Intensive Applications to deepen my understanding while sharing practical lessons with other engineers. If you're also interested in distributed systems, backend engineering, or system design, I'd love to hear your thoughts and discuss what you're learning as well.
